source_provenance: CTA benchmark v0.3; internally authored gold; algorithm family `arrays_binary_search`; behavioral oracle in harness.json.
informal_statement:
Given a sorted slice of integers in nondecreasing order and a target value, return Some(i) where arr[i] == target, or None if the target is absent. If multiple indices match, returning any valid index is acceptable.

[Grid variant 001 — baseline authoritative informal contract for this algorithm family. Same reference implementation and harness as the rest of the family grid; used for parity and verbatim drift checks across releases.]

semantic_units.description: Input precondition: the slice is sorted in nondecreasing order. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: forgetting the precondition entirely
asserting strict ordering instead of nondecreasing

semantic_units.description: Success postcondition: if the return is Some(i), then i is a valid index and arr[i] == target. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: omitting the bounds check
omitting the equality check

semantic_units.description: Failure postcondition: if the return is None, then target does not occur in arr. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: only quantifying over a prefix or suffix
stating the weaker claim that target is not at a specific position

semantic_units.description: Termination: the function terminates on every input (lo..hi shrinks each iteration). (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: stating termination without a variant

semantic_units.description: Uniqueness is NOT required: multiple valid indices exist when duplicates are present. (V001 baseline.)

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (arr : Arr) (t : Int) (i : Nat), binarySearch arr t = some i → i < arr.length ∧ arr.get? i = some t
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: If the function returns Some i, then i is in range and arr[i] equals the target.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (arr : Arr) (t : Int), SortedLE arr → binarySearch arr t = none → ∀ i, i < arr.length → arr.get? i ≠ some t
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: Assuming the input is sorted in nondecreasing order, if the function returns None then the target does not occur anywhere in the slice.

source_provenance: CTA benchmark v0.3; internally authored gold; algorithm family `arrays_max_subarray`; behavioral oracle in harness.json.
informal_statement:
Given a non-empty slice of integers, return the maximum sum over all contiguous non-empty subslices.

[Grid variant 001 — baseline authoritative informal contract for this algorithm family. Same reference implementation and harness as the rest of the family grid; used for parity and verbatim drift checks across releases.]

semantic_units.description: Input precondition: the slice is non-empty. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: omitting the non-emptiness precondition
treating the empty-slice case as returning 0

semantic_units.description: Existential witness: there is a contiguous non-empty subslice whose sum equals the returned value. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: stating only an upper bound without a witness
quantifying over subsequences rather than contiguous subslices

semantic_units.description: Upper-bound property: no contiguous non-empty subslice sums strictly above the returned value. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: missing the maximality condition
quantifying only over subslices of a fixed length

semantic_units.description: Contiguity constraint: the witness is a contiguous range i..j, not a subsequence. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: allowing gaps in the witnessed indices

semantic_units.description: All-negative case: the result equals the maximum single element when every element is negative. (V001 baseline.)

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (arr : Arr), arr ≠ [] → ∃ (i j : Nat), i ≤ j ∧ j ≤ arr.length ∧ i < j ∧ sliceSum arr i j = maxSubarray arr
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: For any non-empty input, there exists a contiguous non-empty subslice whose sum equals the returned maximum.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (arr : Arr) (i j : Nat), arr ≠ [] → i < j → j ≤ arr.length → sliceSum arr i j ≤ maxSubarray arr
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: For any non-empty input, no contiguous non-empty subslice has a sum strictly greater than the returned maximum.

informal_statement:

allowing trailing values to be ignored

common_failure_modes: dropping the capacity constraint
applying strict inequality

semantic_units.description: Value witness: there is a valid selection whose total value equals the returned result. (V001 baseline.)

common_failure_modes: allowing multiplicities (unbounded knapsack)

informal_statement:

common_failure_modes: omitting the strict-increase condition on indices
dropping the 'same relative order' requirement
comparing values element-wise across slices of different lengths

semantic_units.description: Contiguous vs subsequence distinction: the definition allows non-contiguous index selections. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: solving the longest common substring instead of subsequence

reference_obligations.nl_gloss: No common subsequence is strictly longer than the returned value.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (a b : Arr), lcsLength a b = lcsLength b a
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: LCS length is symmetric in its arguments.

source_provenance: CTA benchmark v0.3; internally authored gold; algorithm family `graph_bfs_shortest_path`; behavioral oracle in harness.json.
informal_statement:
Given an unweighted directed graph represented by an adjacency list of n vertices and a source vertex, return a vector dist of length n where dist[v] is Some(k) if the shortest path from source to v uses k edges, and None if v is unreachable.

[Grid variant 001 — baseline authoritative informal contract for this algorithm family. Same reference implementation and harness as the rest of the family grid; used for parity and verbatim drift checks across releases.]

semantic_units.description: Length invariant: dist has length n, equal to the number of vertices. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: omitting the length constraint
stating an upper bound instead of equality

semantic_units.description: Source anchor: dist[source] = Some(0). (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: forgetting to anchor the source distance
allowing dist[source] = Some(k) for k > 0

semantic_units.description: Reachability witness: if dist[v] = Some(k), there is a directed path from source to v using exactly k edges. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: omitting the existential witness
stating the weaker 'v is reachable' without length

semantic_units.description: Minimality: if dist[v] = Some(k), no path uses fewer than k edges. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: confusing the minimal and any-path formulations

semantic_units.description: Unreachability: dist[v] = None iff no path exists from source to v. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: only stating one direction of the iff
conflating 'no path' with 'path of length > bound'

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (adj : Adj) (src : Nat), (bfsShortestPath adj src).length = adj.length
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: The output has the same length as the number of vertices.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (adj : Adj) (src : Nat), src < adj.length → (bfsShortestPath adj src).get? src = some (some 0)
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: The distance from the source to itself is zero.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (adj : Adj) (src v k : Nat), (bfsShortestPath adj src).get? v = some (some k) → ∃ path : List Nat, path.head? = some src ∧ path.getLast? = some v ∧ path.length = k + 1
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: If dist[v] = Some(k), there is a path from source to v with exactly k edges.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (adj : Adj) (src v k : Nat), (bfsShortestPath adj src).get? v = some (some k) → ∀ (path : List Nat), path.head? = some src → path.getLast? = some v → k + 1 ≤ path.length
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: If dist[v] = Some(k), no path from source to v has fewer than k edges.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (adj : Adj) (src v : Nat), (bfsShortestPath adj src).get? v = some none ↔ ¬ ∃ (path : List Nat), path.head? = some src ∧ path.getLast? = some v
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: dist[v] = None if and only if no path from source to v exists.

Benchmark note (graph_bfs_shortest_path_006): BFS distance table (variant 6): emphasize self-loops and zero-length cycles at the source.
Authoring lens: Lens 6 checks minimality clauses remain meaningful when the graph has redundant edges.

semantic_units.description: Length invariant: dist has length n, equal to the number of vertices. (BFS distance table (variant 6): emphasize self-loops and zero-length cycles at the source.)
common_failure_modes: omitting the length constraint
stating an upper bound instead of equality

semantic_units.description: Source anchor: dist[source] = Some(0). (BFS distance table (variant 6): emphasize self-loops and zero-length cycles at the source.)
common_failure_modes: forgetting to anchor the source distance
allowing dist[source] = Some(k) for k > 0

semantic_units.description: Reachability witness: if dist[v] = Some(k), there is a directed path from source to v using exactly k edges. (BFS distance table (variant 6): emphasize self-loops and zero-length cycles at the source.)
common_failure_modes: omitting the existential witness
stating the weaker 'v is reachable' without length

semantic_units.description: Minimality: if dist[v] = Some(k), no path uses fewer than k edges. (BFS distance table (variant 6): emphasize self-loops and zero-length cycles at the source.)
common_failure_modes: confusing the minimal and any-path formulations

semantic_units.description: Unreachability: dist[v] = None iff no path exists from source to v. (BFS distance table (variant 6): emphasize self-loops and zero-length cycles at the source.)
common_failure_modes: only stating one direction of the iff
conflating 'no path' with 'path of length > bound'

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (adj : Adj) (src : Nat), (bfsShortestPath adj src).length = adj.length
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: The output has the same length as the number of vertices.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (adj : Adj) (src : Nat), src < adj.length → (bfsShortestPath adj src).get? src = some (some 0)
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: The distance from the source to itself is zero.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (adj : Adj) (src v k : Nat), (bfsShortestPath adj src).get? v = some (some k) → ∃ path : List Nat, path.head? = some src ∧ path.getLast? = some v ∧ path.length = k + 1
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: If dist[v] = Some(k), there is a path from source to v with exactly k edges.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (adj : Adj) (src v k : Nat), (bfsShortestPath adj src).get? v = some (some k) → ∀ (path : List Nat), path.head? = some src → path.getLast? = some v → k + 1 ≤ path.length
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: If dist[v] = Some(k), no path from source to v has fewer than k edges.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (adj : Adj) (src v : Nat), (bfsShortestPath adj src).get? v = some none ↔ ¬ ∃ (path : List Nat), path.head? = some src ∧ path.getLast? = some v
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: dist[v] = None if and only if no path from source to v exists.

informal_statement:
Given a directed graph with non-negative integer edge weights, a source vertex, and a vertex count n, return a vector dist of length n where dist[v] is the length of the shortest path from source to v, or None if no such path exists.

[Grid variant 001 — baseline authoritative informal contract for this algorithm family. Same reference implementation and harness as the rest of the family grid; used for parity and verbatim drift checks across releases.]

semantic_units.description: Input precondition: every edge's endpoints lie in [0, n) and every weight is non-negative. (V001 baseline.)
Size: the returned dist vector has length n. (V001 baseline.)
Source: dist[source] == Some(0). (V001 baseline.)
Achievability: if dist[v] == Some(d), there exists a path from source to v with total weight d. (V001 baseline.)
Optimality: if dist[v] == Some(d), no path from source to v has total weight less than d. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: stating only a weaker existence claim
asserting only on the shortest-path tree edges

semantic_units.description: Unreachability: if dist[v] == None, there is no path from source to v. (V001 baseline.)
Termination on every valid input. (V001 baseline.)

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (n source : Nat) (g : Graph), (dijkstra n source g).length = n
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: The returned distance table has length n.

reference_obligations.nl_gloss: The distance from the source to itself is zero.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (n source v : Nat) (g : Graph) (d : Nat), NonNegativeWeights g → (dijkstra n source g).get? v = some (some d) → ∀ d', PathWeight g source v d' → d ≤ d'
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When every edge weight is non-negative, if dist[v] == Some(d) then no path from source to v has weight less than d.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (n source v : Nat) (g : Graph) (d : Nat), (dijkstra n source g).get? v = some (some d) → PathWeight g source v d
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: If dist[v] == Some(d), there exists a path from source to v with total weight d.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (n source v : Nat) (g : Graph), (dijkstra n source g).get? v = some none → ¬ ∃ d, PathWeight g source v d
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: If dist[v] == None, there is no path from source to v.

source_provenance: CTA benchmark v0.3; internally authored gold; algorithm family `greedy_coin_change_canonical`; behavioral oracle in harness.json.
informal_statement:
Given a sorted-ascending slice of positive integer coin denominations that forms a canonical system (greedy is optimal), and a non-negative target amount, return a vector `counts` of the same length as the denominations such that `sum(counts[i] * denoms[i]) == amount` and `sum(counts)` is the minimum possible over all such vectors.

[Grid variant 001 — baseline authoritative informal contract for this algorithm family. Same reference implementation and harness as the rest of the family grid; used for parity and verbatim drift checks across releases.]

semantic_units.description: Canonicality precondition: the denomination system must be canonical (greedy yields the optimal count). (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: omitting the canonicality precondition
assuming greedy is always optimal for any denomination system

semantic_units.description: Alignment: the output counts vector has the same length as denoms. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: returning a variable-length vector
dropping trailing zero entries

semantic_units.description: Decomposition: sum(counts[i] * denoms[i]) equals amount. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: stating sum(counts) = amount instead of the weighted sum

semantic_units.description: Optimality: sum(counts) is minimal among all valid decompositions. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: stating only feasibility; admits the zero-count no-op on amount=0 but not in general

semantic_units.description: Non-negativity: every count entry is non-negative. (V001 baseline.)

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (denoms : Denoms) (amt : Nat), (coinChangeCanonical denoms amt).length = denoms.length
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: The returned counts vector aligns with the denomination vector.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (denoms : Denoms) (amt : Nat), Decomposes denoms (coinChangeCanonical denoms amt) amt
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: The returned counts decompose the target amount.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (denoms : Denoms) (amt : Nat) (alt : Counts), Canonical denoms → Decomposes denoms alt amt → (coinChangeCanonical denoms amt).sum ≤ alt.sum
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When the denomination system is canonical, the greedy count sum is at most any competing valid decomposition's sum.

source_provenance: CTA benchmark v0.3; internally authored gold; algorithm family `greedy_interval_scheduling`; behavioral oracle in harness.json.
informal_statement:
Given a slice of closed-open intervals [start, end), return the maximum number of mutually non-overlapping intervals selectable from the input.

[Grid variant 001 — baseline authoritative informal contract for this algorithm family. Same reference implementation and harness as the rest of the family grid; used for parity and verbatim drift checks across releases.]

semantic_units.description: Input precondition: every interval satisfies start < stop. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: allowing start == stop (empty intervals)
omitting the precondition entirely

semantic_units.description: Feasibility: the returned count is witnessed by some subset of the input that is pairwise non-overlapping. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: stating only an upper bound
confusing index selections with value selections

semantic_units.description: Optimality: no pairwise non-overlapping subset of the input is strictly larger than the returned count. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: stating only feasibility (admits the trivial return 0)

semantic_units.description: Endpoint convention: two intervals that share only an endpoint are non-overlapping. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: using strict inequality instead of non-strict at the boundary

semantic_units.description: Empty input: the result is 0 when the input is empty. (V001 baseline.)

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (ivs : Intervals), (∀ i ∈ ivs, i.start < i.stop) → ∃ (sel : List Nat), Feasible ivs sel ∧ sel.length = intervalScheduling ivs
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When every input interval has strictly positive length, there exists a feasible selection whose size equals the returned count.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (ivs : Intervals) (sel : List Nat), (∀ i ∈ ivs, i.start < i.stop) → Feasible ivs sel → sel.length ≤ intervalScheduling ivs
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When every input interval has strictly positive length, no feasible selection is strictly larger than the returned count.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (a b : Interval), a.stop = b.start → NonOverlap a b
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: Intervals touching at a single endpoint are non-overlapping.

informal_statement:
Given a slice of closed-open intervals [start, end), return the maximum number of mutually non-overlapping intervals selectable from the input.

Benchmark note (greedy_interval_scheduling_005): Variant 5 stresses monotonicity or stability properties implied by the reference implementation.
Authoring lens: Lens 5 highlights obligations that must remain checkable against the behavioral oracle.

semantic_units.description: Input precondition: every interval satisfies start < stop. (Variant 5 stresses monotonicity or stability properties implied by the reference implementation.)
common_failure_modes: allowing start == stop (empty intervals)
omitting the precondition entirely

semantic_units.description: Feasibility: the returned count is witnessed by some subset of the input that is pairwise non-overlapping. (Variant 5 stresses monotonicity or stability properties implied by the reference implementation.)
common_failure_modes: stating only an upper bound
confusing index selections with value selections

semantic_units.description: Optimality: no pairwise non-overlapping subset of the input is strictly larger than the returned count. (Variant 5 stresses monotonicity or stability properties implied by the reference implementation.)
common_failure_modes: stating only feasibility (admits the trivial return 0)

semantic_units.description: Endpoint convention: two intervals that share only an endpoint are non-overlapping. (Variant 5 stresses monotonicity or stability properties implied by the reference implementation.)
common_failure_modes: using strict inequality instead of non-strict at the boundary

semantic_units.description: Empty input: the result is 0 when the input is empty. (Variant 5 stresses monotonicity or stability properties implied by the reference implementation.)

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (ivs : Intervals), (∀ i ∈ ivs, i.start < i.stop) → ∃ (sel : List Nat), Feasible ivs sel ∧ sel.length = intervalScheduling ivs
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When every input interval has strictly positive length, there exists a feasible selection whose size equals the returned count.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (ivs : Intervals) (sel : List Nat), (∀ i ∈ ivs, i.start < i.stop) → Feasible ivs sel → sel.length ≤ intervalScheduling ivs
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When every input interval has strictly positive length, no feasible selection is strictly larger than the returned count.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (a b : Interval), a.stop = b.start → NonOverlap a b
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: Intervals touching at a single endpoint are non-overlapping.

source_provenance: CTA benchmark v0.3; internally authored gold; algorithm family `sorting_insertion_sort`; behavioral oracle in harness.json.
informal_statement:
Sort a mutable slice of integers in nondecreasing order using insertion sort. The function mutates its input in place and returns nothing.

[Grid variant 001 — baseline authoritative informal contract for this algorithm family. Same reference implementation and harness as the rest of the family grid; used for parity and verbatim drift checks across releases.]

semantic_units.description: Sortedness: the output list is sorted in nondecreasing order. (V001 baseline.)
Permutation: the output is a permutation of the input (every element preserved, counts preserved). (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: asserting set equality without counting duplicates
asserting length preservation only

semantic_units.description: Termination on every input. (V001 baseline.)
Stability is NOT required and should not be asserted. (V001 baseline.)

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (xs : Arr), SortedLE (insertionSort xs)
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: The output is sorted in nondecreasing order.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (xs : Arr), List.Perm (insertionSort xs) xs
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: The output is a permutation of the input.

informal_statement:
Given a binary search tree whose in-order traversal is strictly ascending and two keys both present in the tree, return the key of the lowest node that has both keys in its subtree.

[Grid variant 001 — baseline authoritative informal contract for this algorithm family. Same reference implementation and harness as the rest of the family grid; used for parity and verbatim drift checks across releases.]

semantic_units.description: BST precondition: the input tree is a valid BST. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: omitting the BST precondition and solving general-tree LCA
assuming strict ordering within equal keys

semantic_units.description: Presence precondition: both query keys are present in the tree. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: omitting the presence precondition
returning None silently when a key is missing

semantic_units.description: Ancestor property: both query keys lie in the subtree rooted at the returned key. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: returning any common ancestor instead of the lowest one
confusing descendant with ancestor

semantic_units.description: Lowest: no proper descendant of the returned node contains both keys. (V001 baseline.)
common_failure_modes: returning the root unconditionally
using LCA of general trees (parent-pointer style) semantics

semantic_units.description: Key equality case: when p == q, the LCA is that same key. (V001 baseline.)

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (t : Tree) (p q a : Int), IsBst t → HasKey t p → HasKey t q → lcaBst t p q = some a → ∃ sub : Tree, IsSubtree sub t ∧ (match sub with | Tree.nil => False | Tree.node _ k _ => k = a) ∧ HasKey sub p ∧ HasKey sub q
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When the input is a BST containing both query keys, the returned key is the root of a subtree containing both queries.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (t : Tree) (p q a : Int), IsBst t → HasKey t p → HasKey t q → lcaBst t p q = some a → ∀ (sub : Tree), IsProperSubtree sub t → (match sub with | Tree.nil => True | Tree.node _ k _ => k = a → False) → HasKey sub p → HasKey sub q → False
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When the input is a BST containing both query keys, no proper subtree that is not rooted at the returned key contains both queries and sits strictly below the LCA.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (t : Tree) (p : Int), IsBst t → HasKey t p → lcaBst t p p = some p
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When the input is a BST containing the key, the LCA of a key with itself is that key.
